--- SOURCE 1: Solving Sustainability with Synthetic Biology - Wyss Institute ---
URL: https://wyss.harvard.edu/news/solving-sustainability-with-synthetic-biology/

SUMMARY:
The Wyss Institute's commitment to solving real-world problems in sustainability, from plastic pollution to textile production to carbon capture.

FULL CONTENT:
![Plants in laboratory glassware on white background. Skincare products and drugs chemical researches concept](https://wyss-prod.imgix.net/app/uploads/2024/04/22145758/plants-in-laboratory-glassware-skincare-products-2023-11-27-05-07-45-utc.jpg?auto=format&q=90&fit=crop&w=400&h=225)

# Solving Sustainability with Synthetic Biology

April 25, 2024

## How harnessing the power of microbes could save us all

By Lindsay Brownell

As humans, we like to think that we are the smartest animals on planet Earth. After all, no other species has invented smartphones, heat pumps, or jet engines. But for all our innovations, we have also created problems on a global scale.  

Take plastic, for example. 

![Solving Sustainability with Synthetic Biology](https://wyss-prod.imgix.net/app/uploads/2024/04/22150604/20240405-401-Park-Construction-Exports-2024.04.05-Kula-Bio-tour-IMG_05811.jpg?w=25&h=19&auto=format&q=10&fit=crop&crop=faces%2Centropy&blur=75)

Humans invented plastic scarcely more than a century ago, but we now produce more than 350 million metric tons of plastic waste per year. That’s the equivalent of 10 million fully loaded garbage trucks. While we have acknowledged that plastic pollution is a problem we need to solve, so far, humans have failed to find a viable solution.  

Microbes, on the other hand, have developed a very simple one in the same timeframe: just eat it. 

Scientists have identified about [400 species of fungi and bacteria worldwide that have developed the ability to eat plastic](https://www.theseacleaners.org/news/mutant-plastic-eating-bacteria-sci-fi-or-plan-b-against-plastic-pollution/). 60% of the plastic-digesting enzymes that these microbes have developed belong to no previously known class in nature, indicating that they are newly evolved.  

Despite developing the capacity to take advantage of this new and plentiful food source, microbes aren’t very good at eating plastic yet – studies estimate that they can only digest about 1% of the plastic they encounter every year.

Fortunately, we don’t have to wait for Nature to speed up this process: we can accelerate it with synthetic biology.  

Synthetic biology – the engineering of living organisms – is a cornerstone of the Wyss Institute’s commitment to solving real-world problems in sustainability, from plastic pollution to textile production to carbon capture. Our world-class scientists and engineers are building the tools needed to rapidly design, test, and iterate microbes with new-to-nature functions, and using those tools to create biology-based solutions to the world’s most pressing problems. 

These new technologies are then shepherded into the [Wyss’ innovation funnel](https://wyss.harvard.edu/how-we-work/), our unique model of developing and de-risking promising ideas to ensure that when they get to market, they are ready to make the biggest possible positive impact.  

![Solving Sustainability with Synthetic Biology](https://wyss-prod.imgix.net/app/uploads/2024/04/22151315/2024.03.14-Reimagining-Sustainability-06508.jpg?w=25&h=17&auto=format&q=10&fit=crop&crop=faces%2Centropy&blur=75)

This model has a track record of launching successful companies. [Circe Bioscience](https://www.circebioscience.com/) is engineering bacteria that can eat greenhouse gases and use their molecular building blocks to produce food-grade fats to decarbonize food production. [Kula Bio](https://kulabio.com/) super-charges microbes to provide nitrogen directly to crops, reducing farmers’ reliance on harmful and expensive synthetic fertilizers. And, bringing things full circle, a startup called Breaking [launched last month](https://wyss.harvard.edu/news/plastic-degradation-company-breaking-emerges-from-stealth-with-naturally-derived-solution-to-degrade-multiple-plastics-with-10-5m-in-seed-funding/) is evolving microbes that can eat plastic waste to address the global pollution problem. 

We’re also exploring new uses for microbes that can help solve the sustainability crisis. Wyss researchers are using them to create the next generation of meat-free proteins, extract rare-earth elements from the ground more sustainably, sense and degrade toxins in the environment, and increase the “weathering” process that sequesters carbon into the oceans.  

Of course, experimenting with living creatures, even those without true brains, creates ethical questions and issues. The Wyss Institute’s in-house ethicist and philosopher, [Jeantine Lunshof](https://wyss.harvard.edu/team/advanced-technology-team/jeantine-lunshof/), holds regular office hours with our researchers to talk about their experiments and ensure that they have thought about and planned to mitigate potential risks.  

While we’re incredibly excited about the [sustainability work](https://wyss.harvard.edu/technologies/sustainability/) going on within our walls, we know that we can’t do it alone. We work closely with the [Syn Bio Hive](https://synbiohive.org/) at Harvard Medical School, co-founded by Wyss Faculty members [Pam Silver](https://wyss.harvard.edu/team/core-faculty/pamela-silver/) and [Mike Springer](https://wyss.harvard.edu/team/associate-faculty/michael-springer/), where scientists with new and exciting ideas about how to use biology to solve sustainability problems form interdisciplinary teams to test those ideas in the lab. We have also [partnered with](https://wyss.harvard.edu/collaboration/collaborative-fund-venture-alliance/) Collab Fund to drive the development of technologies that can make materials more sustainable.  

And we’re just getting started. 

Reach out to [Emily Stoler](https://wyss.harvard.edu/team/advanced-technology-team/emily-stoler/) if you’re interested in getting involved with our sustainability projects.

![Our Sustainable Future](https://i.vimeocdn.com/video/1763754536-ff3ef56d06a0a36d6917f4ef72af2ce92c58a566df5dcd54c18194a303ff4895-d_1920?auto=format&region=us)

# P&G Shares How Biotechnology Fuels Innovation at SynBioBeta 2025

June 15, 2025

The [Global Synthetic Biology Conference](https://www.syntheticbiologysummit.com/), known as SynBioBeta, brings together individuals across a broad range of industries who share a passion for advancing biology and sustainability. At SynBioBeta 2025, P&G R&D leaders joined industry colleagues to discuss strategies for achieving their joint goal of applying synthetic biology to develop high performance products that are sustainable*.*

P&G has a rich history of leveraging biotech for innovation, dating back to our first few branded products derived from tallow waste to make candles, soap and glycerin. Today, we continue to apply biotechnology to unlock superior performance, enhance ingredient sustainability, and deliver high-performing products for hair, health, hygiene, and more.

On the Main Stage, P&G R&D leaders [Lee Ellen Drechsler, PhD](/team/lee-ellen-drechsler/), and [Andy Weatherston](/team/andy-weatherston/) spoke in a featured session, âFrom Startup to Scaleup: Insights to How P&G is Harnessing Bio Innovation for Superior Performance and Sustainability.â They highlighted how synthetic biology can positively impact consumer products. The panel reviewed challengesâboth technical and societalâweâll need to solve to get there, emphasizing how corporate organizations and startups can work together to achieve the functional and sustainable product performance that consumers demand.

![From Startup to Scaleup]()

Several P&G R&D experts were also featured in panel sessions, spanning key themes including innovation, collaboration, and sustainability:

**Bridging the Gap Between Biotech Innovation and Consumer Acceptance**  
**Mikah Coffindaffer, PhD,** R&D Director, joined a conversation with other leaders in the field to discuss how we bridge the gap between biotech innovation and consumer adoptionâbuilding trust, addressing concerns, and effectively communicating the benefits of bio-based products.

**Genes to Giants: Strategic Alliances between SynBio and the Fortune 500**  
**Jose Carlos Garcia-Garcia, PhD,** Senior Director and Research Fellow, participated in a panel exploring how synthetic biology startups can successfully partner with Fortune 500 companies to scale innovation in consumer products.

**Making It Happen: Accelerating the Global Bioeconomy**  
**Amy Trejo, PhD,** Director, Hair Care Sustainability, joined a panel of leaders from industry, government, and the community to explore how to overcome key barriersâlike scaling, regulation, and investment bottlenecksâto unlock the full potential of the bioeconomy and drive real-world impact from synthetic biology.

**Transforming Supply Chains with Designer Proteins**  
**Jose Carlos Garcia-Garcia, PhD,** Senior Director and Research Fellow, together with other panel experts, discussed how engineered proteins can transform supply chains, enhance products, and drive sustainability through strategic partnerships and innovation.

Our team at P&G R&D is committed to unlocking the power of biology to deliver sustainably produced, high-performance materialsâsuch as biopolymers, biosurfactants, bioactives, and biofragrancesâthat meet the needs of people and the planet.

From transforming waste into valuable new feedstocks to embracing the vast potential of microbial diversity, synthetic biology is driving breakthroughs that promote better health and a healthier environment for all. These advances are also driving transformation of global supply networks, making them more resilient, efficient, and secure in the face of growing challenges.

P&G is committed to advancing these efforts to redefine whatâs possible in bio-innovation in the pursuit of a more sustainable future.

[Learn more about P&G R&Dâs expertise in synthetic biology and how we apply it to health & hygiene solutions.](/health-hygiene/)

![Group photo of people standing.]()

--- SOURCE 3: Centre Startups ---
URL: https://www.coesb.com.au/collaborate/startups/

SUMMARY:
Our Centre Startups are real-world examples of how synthetic biology can help solve big problems in energy, the environment, farming, and health.

FULL CONTENT:
Please enable JavaScript in your browser.

![ARC Centre of Excellence in Synthetic Biology](https://www.coesb.com.au/wp-content/uploads/2020/09/logo.png)

Lost your password or first time logging in? Please enter your email address. You will receive a link to create a new password via email.

![ARC Centre of Excellence in Synthetic Biology](https://www.coesb.com.au/wp-content/uploads/2020/09/banner1-1920x900.jpg)

Collaborate

## **Centre startups**

### SWAN Genomics

SWAN is changing the way we sequence DNA with a new approach that goes beyond today’s short-read and long-read methods. Their technology is more flexible, scalable, and cost-effective—opening up exciting possibilities for healthcare and biotech.

Learn more: [https://www.linkedin.com/company/swan-genomics](https://www.linkedin.com/company/swan-genomics/)

### NewEra Bio: Natural & Eco-Friendly Dyes for Fabrics

Newera.bio’s mission is to transform textile dyeing into a bio-based industry that benefits the planet, communities, and future generations. They harness synthetic biology and proprietary platform technologies to produce low carbon and high-performance dyes that are cost-effective and eco-friendly.

Learn more: [https://www.newera.bio](https://www.newera.bio/)

### Wild Yeast Zoo

Wild Yeast Zoo is an innovative platform committed to the Nagoya Protocol, building a diverse library of wild Australian microbes to create nature-based solutions for sustainable industrial innovation. They focus on developing bio-based applications across industries, from fermented beverages to agriculture, with a strong emphasis on environmental responsibility and scalability. Through collaboration and creative approaches, they aim to accelerate transformative change for a brighter future.

Learn more: [https://www.wildyeastzoo.com](https://www.wildyeastzoo.com/)

### Levur: Unlocking the Next Generation of Oils

Levur is transforming how industries produce oils, offering a sustainable, scalable alternative to palm oil—a resource found in over 50% of supermarket products and linked to deforestation, biodiversity loss, and high emissions. Using synthetic biology and precision fermentation, they brew palm oil derivatives in a controlled environment, enabling tailored oil profiles for cosmetics, nutraceuticals, and food. Their process helps industries comply with sustainability regulations like the EU’s 2026 deforestation ban while reducing environmental impact. Partnering with leading FMCG companies, they are scaling towards a nature-positive economy, empowering businesses to meet sustainability goals and protect biodiversity for future generations.

Learn more: [https://www.levur.com.au](https://www.levur.com.au/)

### EntoZyme: Building a Bio-Future

EntoZyme is transforming sustainable waste management and biomanufacturing industries with the use of transgenic Black Soldier Flies.

Learn more: [https://www.entozyme.com](https://www.entozyme.com/)

### Number 8 Bio

Number 8 Bio pioneers transformative change in the AgriTech and CleanTech sectors, providing innovative and sustainable methods to reduce livestock emissions. Its product BetterFeed™ is expected to achieve a remarkable 98 percent reduction in methane emissions while helping cattle gain 10% percent more weight on the same amount of feed.

Key recent milestones include the commencement of first cattle trials and the active construction of a pilot plant in Lane Cove, emphasising scalability.

Learn more: [https://www.number8.bio](https://www.number8.bio/)

## **LIFE REIMAGINED**

We are a world-leading research centre whose goal is to take us beyond what any single, existing microbe on earth can do.

##### 

![](https://www.coesb.com.au/wp-content/uploads/2022/05/logo.svg)

--- SOURCE 4: Top 20 Innovative Synthetic Biology Companies in the US ---
URL: https://www.scispot.com/blog/top-20-innovative-synthetic-biology-companies-in-the-us

SUMMARY:
Founded in 2021 and based in San Francisco, California, Pando Biosciences integrates machine learning and synthetic biology to accelerate enzyme discovery and optimization. Founded in 2016 and headquartered in Alameda, California, Checkerspot applies synthetic biology to materials science, developing bio-based materials for sports equipment, textiles, and personal care products. As synthetic biology research becomes increasingly data-driven, managing lab automation, reproducibility, and compliance is critical for biotech companies. Scispot’s Lab Operating System (LabOSᵀᴹ) is designed to support synthetic biology companies, helping them streamline data workflows, inventory tracking, and collaboration in a unified digital platform. The future of synthetic biology is being shaped by automation, data-driven insights, and AI-powered lab systems, paving the way for sustainable and innovative biotech solutions.

Trends

Top 20 Innovative Synthetic Biology Companies in the US
=======================================================

Olivia Wilson

4 min read

June 30, 2025

 •

Tag

[![Image 5: Basiic Maill iicon ](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/61c463c70889dd1fe2be377f/67ba8110a9df8a92164701fb_Basic%20Mail.svg)](https://www.scispot.com/blog/top-20-innovative-synthetic-biology-companies-in-the-us#)[![Image 6](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/61c463c70889dd1fe2be377f/67ba8110a9df8a92164701fe_Group%20452.svg)](https://www.scispot.com/blog/top-20-innovative-synthetic-biology-companies-in-the-us#)[![Image 7](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/61c463c70889dd1fe2be377f/67ba8110a9df8a92164701fc_Group%20454.svg)](https://www.scispot.com/blog/top-20-innovative-synthetic-biology-companies-in-the-us#)

![Image 8: Top 20 Innovative Synthetic Biology Companies in the US](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/61c463c70889ddb780be379d/6265c00da9da5bd8b531369d_Revised.gif)[Post by](https://www.scispot.com/blog/top-20-innovative-synthetic-biology-companies-in-the-us#)

Synthetic biology applies engineering principles to biological systems, driving advancements in medicine, agriculture, and industrial biotechnology. As of 2025, the number of synthetic biology companies in the United States has grown substantially, fueled by progress in DNA synthesis, genome editing, and biomanufacturing.

The global synthetic biology market is projected to exceed $50 billion by 2025, reflecting increasing investments and technological advancements in the field. This list highlights 20 innovative companies leading the way in this dynamic sector.

‍

Here are 20 of the top innovative synthetic biology startups in the US chosen by us at Scispot:

[**Ginkgo Bioworks**](https://www.ginkgo.bio/)

Founded in 2008 and headquartered in Boston, Massachusetts, Ginkgo Bioworks is one of the most well-known names in synthetic biology. The company specializes in custom organism design and bioengineering, using its advanced biofoundry to develop microbes tailored for pharmaceuticals, agriculture, and industrial manufacturing. By leveraging automation, artificial intelligence, and high-throughput screening, Ginkgo accelerates the development of engineered organisms that contribute to sustainable and efficient bioprocesses across multiple industries.

‍**‍**

[**Mammoth Biosciences**](https://mammoth.bio/)

Founded in 2017 in Brisbane, California, Mammoth Biosciences is revolutionizing CRISPR-based diagnostics and gene editing. The company’s proprietary detector platform leverages CRISPR to create rapid, accurate, and accessible disease diagnostics. In addition to diagnostics, Mammoth is expanding into therapeutic applications, aiming to develop gene-editing solutions for precision medicine. Their technology has broad applications in infectious disease detection, oncology, and genetic disorder treatments.**‍**

‍

[**Tessera Therapeutics**](https://www.tesseratherapeutics.com/)

Tessera Therapeutics, based in Cambridge, Massachusetts, was founded in 2018 and is pioneering RNA-based gene writing. Unlike traditional gene-editing technologies, gene writing enables precise DNA modifications without cutting, offering a safer and more targeted approach to genome engineering. This next-generation genetic medicine has vast potential in treating genetic diseases, regenerative medicine, and engineered cell therapies.

‍

[**Asimov**](https://www.asimov.com/)

Asimov, headquartered in Boston, Massachusetts, was founded in 2017 and focuses on mammalian synthetic biology and gene circuit engineering. The company is building an advanced genetic circuit programming platform to control gene expression in mammalian cells. This allows for the development of programmable cell therapies, advanced biologics, and gene regulation systems, significantly advancing biotechnology and synthetic biology-based drug discovery.

‍

[![Image 9](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/61c463c70889ddb780be379d/66c4b8362111c13e82ec0371_66c4b76b82e2a6acb12bb5c9_4.png)](https://www.scispot.com/demo)
‍**‍**

[**Sherlock Biosciences**](https://sherlock.bio/)

Sherlock Biosciences, established in 2019 in Watertown, Massachusetts, is a leading developer of CRISPR-based molecular diagnostics. The company’s SHERLOCK™ and INSPECTR™ platforms allow for rapid and accurate detection of pathogens, genetic disorders, and biomarkers. These tools enhance accessibility and affordability in molecular diagnostics, providing healthcare solutions for point-of-care testing, pandemic preparedness, and decentralized diagnostics.

‍**‍**

[**Pando Biosciences**](https://www.pando.bio/)

Founded in 2021 and based in San Francisco, California, Pando Biosciences integrates machine learning and synthetic biology to accelerate enzyme discovery and optimization. Their platform enhances the development of biocatalysts for pharmaceuticals, agriculture, and industrial bioprocessing, making processes more sustainable and efficient. By using AI-driven modeling, Pando’s approach reduces the time and costs required for enzyme engineering.

‍**‍**

[**Volta Labs**](https://www.voltalabs.com/)

Founded in 2018 in Cambridge, Massachusetts, Volta Labs has developed a digital fluidics platform that automates sample preparation for genomic and molecular biology applications. Their technology improves efficiency, reproducibility, and data accuracy in research laboratories. By enabling automated liquid handling, Volta Labs simplifies next-generation sequencing (NGS), CRISPR workflows, and synthetic biology applications.

‍

[![Image 10](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/61c463c70889ddb780be379d/686263db7faf467bac811858_top-20-innovative-synthetic-biology-companies-in-the-us.webp)](https://www.scispot.com/demo)
‍

[**Synthego**](https://www.synthego.com/)

Founded in 2012 and headquartered in Menlo Park, California, Synthego is a leader in genome engineering solutions. The company offers synthetic RNA, CRISPR reagents, and AI-driven bioinformatics tools, facilitating precise and scalable genome editing. By providing high-throughput gene-editing capabilities, Synthego is helping researchers accelerate therapeutic development, functional genomics, and synthetic biology innovations.

‍**‍**

[**Locus Biosciences**](https://www.locus-bio.com/)

Locus Biosciences, based in Morrisville, North Carolina, was founded in 2015 and is at the forefront of CRISPR-based bacterial therapeutics. The company specializes in engineered bacteriophages that selectively target and eliminate antibiotic-resistant bacteria, offering a novel approach to treating multi-drug-resistant infections. This CRISPR-Cas3 technology has the potential to revolutionize how bacterial infections are treated, reducing reliance on broad-spectrum antibiotics.

‍

[**Pivot Bio**](https://www.pivotbio.com/)

Founded in 2011 and headquartered in Berkeley, California, Pivot Bio is transforming agriculture through microbial nitrogen fixation. Their engineered microbes naturally fix nitrogen in soil, reducing reliance on synthetic fertilizers. By improving soil health and crop resilience, Pivot Bio helps farmers enhance yields while reducing environmental impact, making agriculture more sustainable.

‍

[**Geltor**](https://geltor.com/)

Founded in 2015 and based in San Leandro, California, Geltor focuses on animal-free protein production for use in cosmetics, nutraceuticals, and food products. Using precision fermentation, Geltor creates bioidentical proteins without requiring animal-derived ingredients. Their sustainable protein solutions have gained adoption in skincare, alternative collagen production, and dietary supplements, making them a leader in ethical and bio-based protein manufacturing.

‍

[**Perfect Day**](https://perfectday.com/)

Founded in 2014 and headquartered in Berkeley, California, Perfect Day is pioneering animal-free dairy proteins using precision fermentation. Their proteins are nutritionally equivalent to those found in cow’s milk but lactose-free, antibiotic-free, and environmentally sustainable. Perfect Day has partnered with food and beverage brands to create dairy-free ice cream, cheese, and protein powders, driving the future of sustainable food production.

‍

[![Image 11](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/61c463c70889ddb780be379d/66e084f21dd73d13511e5032_64837a790ea21e3dc915a343_JR_PgrkfgyDQvkpP8351_J-h1Nz5aIlkEHrlZ71PFmkL2BpkUfitEjvxNm9srKtqlIV8kBRJX4c8zSr5Sqj6I0nh6d5JtHcGnSgwooq55_1eqj11Xy2nEAbuUWWHIZRlBEYes1Q-woYSrsc6Clpjfwk.png)](https://www.scispot.com/demo)
‍

‍[**Arzeda**](https://www.arzeda.com/)

Arzeda, founded in 2008 and based in Seattle, Washington, specializes in computational protein design and enzyme engineering. By combining AI-driven modeling and synthetic biology, Arzeda develops customized enzymes for industrial applications. Their technology has been applied in biodegradable plastics, agricultural crop enhancement, and sustainable chemical production, reducing the reliance on petroleum-based products.

‍

[‍**Caribou Biosciences**](https://www.cariboubio.com/)

Founded in 2011 and based in Berkeley, California, Caribou Biosciences is a leader in CRISPR-based genome editing for therapeutics. Their precision gene-editing platform is advancing treatments for oncology and immune disorders, with applications in CAR-T cell therapies, regenerative medicine, and rare genetic disease treatments. Caribou’s innovations have made them a major player in gene therapy advancements.

‍

[**Twist Bioscience**](https://www.twistbioscience.com/)

Twist Bioscience, based in South San Francisco, California, was founded in 2013 and has become a major player in the DNA synthesis industry. The company employs a silicon-based DNA synthesis process, enabling scalable and cost-effective production of synthetic DNA. This technology supports research in gene therapy, precision medicine, and synthetic biology applications, allowing researchers to develop novel biomolecules and advance genetic engineering at an unprecedented pace.

‍

[**Lygos**](https://lygos.com/)

Founded in 2010 and based in Berkeley, California, Lygos engineers microbial strains to produce bio-based specialty chemicals as alternatives to petroleum-based ingredients. Their synthetic biology platform enables the sustainable production of organic acids and specialty chemicals used in agriculture, personal care, and industrial manufacturing. Lygos focuses on low-carbon, non-toxic alternatives to conventional chemical synthesis.

‍

[**Zymochem**](https://www.zymochem.com/)

Zymochem, founded in 2017 and headquartered in San Leandro, California, is focused on carbon-efficient biomanufacturing. Their engineered microbes prevent carbon loss during fermentation, enabling the production of bio-based materials, biodegradable plastics, and green chemicals. Their approach reduces CO₂ emissions while creating high-value, sustainable bioproducts for industrial applications.

‍

‍[**Inscripta**](https://www.inscripta.com/)

Founded in 2015 and based in Boulder, Colorado, Inscripta develops next-generation genome editing technology that allows for precise and automated DNA modifications. Their Onyx™ digital genome engineering platform enables researchers to edit microbial genomes at an unprecedented scale, accelerating biomanufacturing and synthetic biology research. Inscripta’s technology is critical for advancing custom enzyme development, metabolic engineering, and industrial biotechnology.

‍**‍**

[**Checkerspot**](https://checkerspot.com/)

Founded in 2016 and headquartered in Alameda, California, Checkerspot applies synthetic biology to materials science, developing bio-based materials for sports equipment, textiles, and personal care products. Their engineered microalgae-derived oils serve as sustainable replacements for petroleum-based polyurethanes and chemicals, helping industries transition toward eco-friendly materials.

‍**‍**

[**Ginkgo Enzymes**](https://industrialbio.ginkgo.bio/)

A division of Ginkgo Bioworks, Ginkgo Enzymes was launched to specialize in custom enzyme discovery and development. Using AI-driven screening and synthetic biology, Ginkgo Enzymes creates highly efficient biocatalysts for applications in biopharma, food processing, and industrial chemical production. Their high-throughput enzyme engineering accelerates biomanufacturing efficiency across multiple sectors.

‍

**Why Synthetic Biology Companies Should Choose Scispot as Their Lab Software**
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

As synthetic biology research becomes increasingly data-driven, managing lab automation, reproducibility, and compliance is critical for biotech companies. Scispot’s Lab Operating System (LabOSᵀᴹ) is designed to support synthetic biology companies, helping them streamline data workflows, inventory tracking, and collaboration in a unified digital platform.

##### **Key Benefits of Scispot:**

*   **Automated Data Management:** Centralizes experimental data, ensuring better reproducibility and reducing errors.
*   **AI-Powered Workflow Automation:** Speeds up experiment planning, sample tracking, and regulatory documentation.
*   **Compliance & Security:** Built-in adherence to ISO, HIPAA, CAP, and GDPR standards, keeping data secure and audit-ready.
*   **Scalability & Integrations:** Seamlessly connects with lab instruments, cloud databases, and external bioinformatics tools, allowing biotech companies to scale operations effortlessly.

By integrating Scispot’s LabOSᵀᴹ, synthetic biology startups and enterprises can focus on breakthrough research while automating their lab processes efficiently.

‍

**Conclusion**
--------------

The synthetic biology sector is growing rapidly, driven by advancements in genome engineering, sustainable biomanufacturing, and molecular diagnostics. These 20 innovative companies are at the forefront of transforming industries ranging from biopharma to materials science and agriculture.

As research complexity increases, digital lab platforms like Scispot will play a crucial role in enabling biotech companies to accelerate discoveries, maintain compliance, and scale their operations. The future of synthetic biology is being shaped by automation, data-driven insights, and AI-powered lab systems, paving the way for sustainable and innovative biotech solutions.

‍

enriched_points.expanded_content: Synthetic biology start-ups prioritize eco-friendly solutions, utilizing engineered organisms to combat issues like plastic waste, carbon emissions, and unsustainable agricultural practices, promoting a healthier planet.
These companies leverage cutting-edge techniques such as genome editing, synthetic pathways, and advanced bioengineering to create high-performance, sustainable products across various industries.
Start-ups aim to tackle pressing global issues, including food security, climate change, and pollution, by developing biological solutions that are efficient, scalable, and environmentally friendly.

Collaboration between synthetic biology firms and established industries, like agriculture and pharmaceuticals, enhances innovation, accelerates commercialization, and fosters the adoption of sustainable practices.

The synthetic biology sector is poised for significant growth, projected to surpass $50 billion by 2025, driven by increasing investments, technological advancements, and rising consumer demand for sustainability.
